coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of superoxide anion with polystyrene@nanoscale coordination polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yonghai
Hao, Juan
Hu, Dongqin
Zeng, Mulan
Li, Ping
Li, Hongbo
Chen, Lili
Tan, Hongliang
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description:
A novel ratio fluorescence sensor for superoxide anion (O2
−) detection was developed with suffocated polystyrene nanoparticles (PS-SO3H)@terbium (Tb)/guanine (G) nanoscale coordination polymers (PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs). The abundant sulfonyl of PS-SO3H are served as binding points for the spontaneous self-assembly of Tb3+ and G on the surface of PS-SO3H to form PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensor. The formation of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs was verified by detailed characterization (i.e., scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy). The obtained senor exhibits the characteristic fluorescence emission of Tb3+ and PS-SO3H. The fluorescence of PS-SO3H is slightly weaken as a reference and the fluorescence of Tb3+ is largely weaken in the presence of O2
−, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent sensor toward the O2
−. The ratio of fluorescence density linearly decreases with the increasing of O2
− concentration in the range of 10.12 nM-6.0μM with a detection limit as low as 3.4nM. This ratiometric fluorescent sensor also shows good selectivity toward O2
−.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PS-SO3H@Tb/G
Superoxide anion
Suffocated polystyrene nanoparticles
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title:
Fluorescence response of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs toward O2
−
Characterization of PS-SO3H particles and PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs

Detection of O2
− based on the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21465014 and 21465015 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province ( 20142BAB203101 and 20143ACB21016 ). The Ministry of Education by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20133604110002 ) and the Ground Plan of Science and Technology Projects of Jiangxi Educational Committee ( KJLD14023 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs and the principle of fluorescence detection toward O2
−.
SEM images of PS (A), PS-SO3H (B), PS-SO3H@Tb/G1 NCPs (C), PS-SO3H@Tb/G2 NCPs (D), PS-SO3H@Tb/G3 NCPs (E) and PS-SO3H@Tb/G4 NCPs (F).
TEM images of PS-SO3H (A,B) and PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs (C,D).

Fluorescence emission spectra of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs with different concentration (A). The plot of fluorescence intensity at 545nm versus the concentration of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs (B). Inset is the plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (I 544/I 463) versus the concentration of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs (upon the excitation at 270nm).

Fluorescence emission spectra of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs in the presence of O2
− with different concentration (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 18, 28, 38 and 48μM, pH=8, 50mM Tris-HCl) (A). The plot of I 544/I 463 versus the O2
− concentration (B). Inset: the calibration line. The PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs concentration is 0.42g/L and the excitation wavelength is 275nm.

Selectivity test of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs (0.42g/L) for O2
− detection over some reactive oxygen species (pH=8, 50mM Tris-HCl buffer). The concentrations of all the compounds are the same (5.0μM) and measurements are performed under identical conditions.
Comparison of sensing performance of different Cu2+detection.

row.entry: CdTe@ZnS quantum dots with cobalt tetraanmino-phthalocyanine

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of superoxide anion with polystyrene@nanoscale coordination polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A novel ratio fluorescence sensor for superoxide anion (O2
−) detection was developed with suffocated polystyrene nanoparticles (PS-SO3H)@terbium (Tb)/guanine (G) nanoscale coordination polymers (PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs). The abundant sulfonyl of PS-SO3H are served as binding points for the spontaneous self-assembly of Tb3+ and G on the surface of PS-SO3H to form PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensor. The formation of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs was verified by detailed characterization (i.e., scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy). The obtained senor exhibits the characteristic fluorescence emission of Tb3+ and PS-SO3H. The fluorescence of PS-SO3H is slightly weaken as a reference and the fluorescence of Tb3+ is largely weaken in the presence of O2
−, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent sensor toward the O2
−. The ratio of fluorescence density linearly decreases with the increasing of O2
− concentration in the range of 10.12 nM-6.0μM with a detection limit as low as 3.4nM. This ratiometric fluorescent sensor also shows good selectivity toward O2
−.

ce:section.ce:para:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is the important intermediate for many toxicological mechanisms pathogens [1–4]. Superoxide anion (O2
−), the precursor of ROS, is the single electron reeducates of molecular oxygen, so understanding the relation between O2
− and diseases is of great importance [4,5]. O2
− can transform into other ROS, and is one of the most important radicals in the body. With aging, stress, and pathological conditions, organism removal ability is weakened and resulted in excessive O2
−, which causes the peroxidation of body lipid and induces cancer, cardiovascular disease, hepatitis, etc [6,7]. Thus it is very important to design analytical strategies to detect O2
− with high selectivity and accuracy in live cells, tissues and organisms [2,4,8,9].

Over the past few years, fluorescence sensor has received extensive attention owing to its easy operation, high sensitivity and rapid response [2,3,10–12]. More and more efforts have been devoted to develop efficient fluorescent probe, which is crucial for high sensitivity and selectivity [13–15]. Although organic fluorescence probes do very well, the synthetic steps are always tedious and most of the organic molecules have poor solubility and stability, even biotoxicity for cell, thus limit the application of organic fluorescent probes in biological systems [16]. Besides, organic fluorescence probe are prone to light bleaching and the testing process are easy to suffer from the interference of background fluorescence, therefore it is not suitable to detect O2
−.

Nanoscale coordination polymers (NCPs) constructed by using biological molecules as the bridging ligands to build fluorescence rare earth ions as a fluorescent probe can overcome these defects [14,17,18]. However, most NCPs are of globular or amorphous structure [19] and their outer surfaces are tight and smooth [20]. It is often difficult for object molecules to enter into the inner surface and interact with the binding sites of the surface outside, which reduce the efficiency of fluorescence sensing materials. Core-shell structured NCPs have become hot area of research in recent years due to their large specific surface area and adjustable size of the shell [21–23]. For example, Moonhyun used a template to synthesize core-shell structured NCPs with controllable shell thickness, which increased the available specific surface area greatly [13,24] . However, most NCPs–based sensors are carried out in organic solution, and tests in the aqueous solution are rarely reported. In addition, most NCPs sensors used to measure the concentration of substances depend on the changes of fluorescence intensity, which will encounter the challenge of the intensity fluctuation inevitably, such as the source and detector drift, the influence of environment, the probe change especially in very low concentrations of analyze concentration. This problem can be solved by the ratiometric fluorescence method via measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio of two different wavelengths [25–27] . The NCPs ratio fluorescent sensor has been used to detect the pH and the body temperature [28,29].

Herein, we established a novel ratio fluorescence sensor to detect O2
− based on suffocated polystyrene nanoparticles (PS-SO3H)@terbium (Tb)/guanine (G) NCPs (PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs) as the fluorescence probe. The PS-SO3H not only used as a template to induce the growth of Tb/G into the core-shell structured NCPs, but also could be used as the reference fluorescence parts for the novel probe, while the O2
− responsive Tb/G NCPs was the recognized parts. The fluorescence of Tb/G NCPs would be quenched markedly by O2
− because O2
− would selectively oxidize G to result in the third line state be damaged and could not sensitize the luminescence of rare earth ions. The results showed the ratio of I 544 /I 463 was quenched lineally in the O2
− concentration range of 10.12 nM-6.0μM with the detection limit as low as 3.4nM. In addition, the fluorescent sensor showed good selectivity.

Tb(NO3)3 was purchased from Raven's rare earth metal materials Co. Ltd. (Baotou, China). 2, 6-Pyridine acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. G and 2-amino-2- (hydroxymethyl)-1, 3-propanediol (Tris) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co.Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pyrogallol was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Ethanol was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water purified by a Millipore-Q system (18.2MΩcm−1).

50mL of pure water was added to three-necked flask and nitrogen was passed though. 30min later, 3.0mL styrene solution was added into the three-necked flask under vigorous stirring [30]. After the temperature rose to 70°C, 0.50g potassium persulfate was added and reacted for 3h under nitrogen at 70°C and stirring. Next, PS particles were collected by centrifugation, washing three times by ethanol and pure water, and drying at 60°C for 6h. Then, 0.20g PS particles were added into 40mL concentrated sulfuric acid and reacted for 2h under vigorous stirring and 40°C to obtain PS-SO3H. The PS-SO3H particles were collected by centrifugation, washing by ethanol and pure water for further use. The synthetic route of PS-SO3H particles was schemically shown in Fig. 1 .

0.0066g PS-SO3H particles was immersed into 4.0mL ethanol solution of Tb(NO3)3, and the mixture was heated to 70°C for 20min under stirring. The precipitates were collected though centrifugation, and then were dispersed in 4.0mL ethanol solution of G. The mixture was heated to 70°C for 30min to obtain the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs. The growth process is defined as a growth cycle and the resulted PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs were called as PS-SO3H@Tb/G1 NCPs. Repeating the above growth process, the PS-SO3H@Tb/G2 NCPs, PS-SO3H@Tb/G3 NCPs and PS-SO3H@Tb/G4 NCPs were prepared, respectively. The synthetic route of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs was also shown in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Fluorescence response of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs toward O2
−
ce:section.ce:para:
6.0mg PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs powder were added into 3.0mL 50mM of Tris-HCl buffer (pH=8), and then incubated for 30min in 25°C water-bath. 10μL PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs suspension were incubated with 90μL of Tris-HCl buffer (pH=8, 50mM) for 30min at room temperature, and finally the concentration of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs was 0.20mgmL−1. In order to investigate the scavenging activity of O2
− for PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs, a pyrogallol autoxidation test was carried out. The 0.0032g pyrogallol was incubated in 10mL Tris-HCl (pH=8, 50mM) for 20min at 25°C, and then pyrogallol auto-oxidation generated a lots of O2
−. Immediately, it was used for the above experiment [31]. The mechanism of fluorescence response of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs to O2
− was also shown in Fig. 1.

The measurements of excitation and emission spectra were performed using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer and the emission spectra were monitored at a 375-nm excitation wavelength. An Avatar 360 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) was used for recording the FTIR spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a JEM-2010 (HR) microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using an XL30 ESEM-FEG SEM. Uv–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by Uv–vis spectrophotometer meter (Hitachi U-3900H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PS-SO3H particles and PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM image of PS particles showed that some spherical particles with a diameter of 475nm well distributed on surface, indicating good dispersion in aqueous solution (Fig. 2 A). After they were suffonated, the average diameter of PS-SO3H was increased to 500nm (Fig. 2B). Besides, the surface of PS-SO3H became rougher as compared with that of PS particles. The results clearly indicated that the PS particles have been successfully sulfonated to provied a lot of sulfonyl group on the surface. After Tb/G NCPs grew on the surface of PS-SO3H, some small particles appeared and the average diameter of PS-SO3H@Tb/G1 NCPs obviously increased as shown in Fig. 2C. As the growth cycles increased, these particles became larger and larger and some PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs began to blend together (Fig. 2D and E). After four growth cycles, more and more PS-SO3H@Tb/G4 NPCs aggregated into clusters as shown in Fig. 2F. So the PS-SO3H@Tb/G3 NCPs were used in the following experiments owing to their good dispersion.

Fig. 3 showed the TEM images of PS-SO3H and PS-SO3H@Tb/G3 NCPs. As shown in Fig. 3A and B, an obvious layer of −SO3H groups were coated on the surface of PS-SO3H, proving the PS-SO3H was successfully synthesized. A larger number of −SO3H groups could coordinate with Tb3+ and then further coordinated with G to form PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs. As shown in Fig. 3C and D, the Tb/G NCPs distributed evenly on the PS-SO3H surface, and the thickness of the shell was about 10nm, indicating the formation of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs. The EDS image indicated the C, N, O, S and Tb elements in PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs, further confirming that the successful synthesis of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs (Fig. S1, Supporting Information).

The FTIR was used to further characterize PS-SO3H particles and PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs. The FTIR spectra of PS, PS-SO3H, PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs and PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs+pyrogallol were shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). As for PS, the characteristic peaks of benzene ring at about 1612cm−1, 1585cm−1, 1500cm−1 and 1450cm−1, and the CH stretching vibration at 3000–3100cm−1 were observed, indicating PS composites was synthesized successfully. FTIR spectra of PS-SO3H showed that the SO asymmetric stretching vibration absorption peak at 1176cm−1, indicating the PS-SO3H was synthesized. FTIR spectra of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs showed that the SO stretching vibration absorption peak shifted from 1176cm−1 to 1108cm−1, and the absorption peak intensity increased accordingly, indicating the sulfonyl of PS-SO3H had coordinated with Tb3+ to form the Tb/G NCPs shell. Besides, the characteristic peaks of G were observed at 1000–1300cm−1. However, after pyrogallol was added, the peak of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs at 1108cm−1 disappeared, which suggested that the produced O2
− oxidized the G in PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs into 8-hydroxy guanine.

The preparation of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs probe and sensing mechanism was shown in Fig. 1. The PS particles were prepared by polymerization of styrene. After suffonation, the sulfonyl of PS-SO3H would coordinate with Tb3+ which gathered in PS-SO3H. These binding sites could result in the self-assembly of Tb3+ and G on the surface of PS-SO3H. Here the PS-SO3H not only used as a template to promote the growth of Tb/G into the NCPs core-shell, but also could be used as the ratio fluorescent sensing characteristic absorption peak. The obtained PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs showed one strong emission peak at 463nm originated from PS-SO3H emission and four emission peaks at 488nm, 544nm, 585nm and 620nm from characteristic emission of Tb3+ under the excitation of 270nm (Fig. 4 ). The G molecules could sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+. After the O2
− was added, the G would be selectively oxidized by O2
− into G triplet and could not sensitize the luminescence of Tb3+, causing the fluorescence quenching of Tb/G NCPs. While the PS-SO3H emission peak only decreased slightly. Therefore, the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs could be used as ratio fluorescence sensors to detect O2
− [32].

To gain a low detection limit and a high sensitivity for O2
− detection, the condition for the sensing of O2
− based on PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs were optimized. As far as we know, the sensitivity of the sensor always depends on pH, concentration of probe and excitation wavelength. The effect of pH and excitation on the sensitivity can be overcome by the ratiometric strategy. Thus only the probe concentration was explored in the current work. As shown in Fig. 4A, as the concentration of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs increased from 0.024g/L to 0.54g/L, the intensity of emission spectrum was increased accordingly. In the concentration range of 0.024-0.54g/L, the intensity of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs emission peak showed a good linear relationship (Fig. 4B). Besides, the I5
44/I 463 almost kept constant (Inset in Fig. 4B). The result indicated that the ratio fluorescent sensor was a good way to overcome environmental changes. The fluorescence of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs was very stable as there were almost no changes in the fluorescence intensity for more than half a month (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The result demonstrated that the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs exhibited excellent optical stability and were well dispersed in aqueous solution. The optical stability and disperibility made the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs possess the potential as florescence biosensing platform. In order to obtain better detection sensitivity, the 0.42g/L was chosen as the optimum concentration of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs based on the ratio fluorescence detection of O2
− in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Detection of O2
− based on the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the experimental conditions, O2
− with different concentration was gradually added into Tris-HCl (pH=8, 50mM) containing 0.42g/L PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs and then the fluorescence intensity was measured (Fig. 5 ). With the increase of O2
− concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Tb/G NCPs decreased greatly and the PS-SO3H emission peak was slightly weakened as indicated in Fig. 5A. The I 544/I 463 was plotted versus the concentration of O2
− in Fig. 5B. As the concentration of O2
− varied from 10.12nM to 6.0μM, the I 544/I 463 showed a good linear correlation with O2
− concentration. The detection limit was calculated to be 3.4nM based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3. To exhibit the advantage of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensor, the performances of different detection methods were compared in Table 1 . By comparing, the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensor showed a lower detection limit and a wider detection range because of the unique structure and the ratio fluorescence method.

In order to assess the selectivity of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensors as a sensing platform for O2
− detection, we measured the change of fluorescence intensity of PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs in the presence of some other active radicals, including H2O2, 1O2, HOCl,。OH and NO, and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . Only O2
− resulted in the fluorescence quenching of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs, and other radicals almost did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs, indicating the high selectivity of the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs sensor for O2
− detection. The highly specific selectivity could be ascribed to the higher reactivity between O2
− and G than other radicals.

In summary, the PS-SO3H@Tb/G NCPs were synthesized and applied as ratio fluorescence sensors for detecting O2
− successfully. The PS-SO3H not only used as a template for the synthesis of Tb/G NCPs but also emitted characteristic fluorescence as a reference. The core-shell structure provided a large surface area in order to improve the sensitivity for O2
− detection. This method was expected to be expanded to other synthesis of core-shell structured NCPs, as well as to the cell's O2
− detection. The present fluorescent sensor exhibited high selectivity, long-term stability against light illumination and pH, good cell permeability, low cytotoxicity, and high biocompatibility. The senor established favorable fluorescence response in the O2
− concentration of 10.12 nM-6.0μM with a very low detection limit of 3.4nm and a high selectivity. This work has not only established a reliable and accurate approach to detect O2
− but also provided a methodology to design the radiometric sensors for other ROS and related species, which might play critical roles in the biological and pathological events.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21465014 and 21465015), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20142BAB203101 and 20143ACB21016). The Ministry of Education by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20133604110002) and the Ground Plan of Science and Technology Projects of Jiangxi Educational Committee (KJLD14023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.181.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yonghai Song received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. He is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His current research interests focus on biosensors.

Juan Hao received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2014 from Jiangxi Science and technology Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Dongqin Hu received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2012 from Gannan Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Mulan Zeng received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2014 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master’s degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.
Ping Li received her master degree in 2004 from University of Science and Technology of China. He is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. His research interest is biosensors.
Hongbo Li received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Hunan University, China. He is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. His research interest is biosensors.

Lili Chen received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Nanchang University, China. She is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Hongliang Tan received his Ph.D. from Southeast University, China. He is currently working as an associate professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His current research interest is focused on fluorescence sensor.

Li Wang received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. She is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. Her current research interest is focused on biosensors.
